_comment: Ground truth annotation — CONSORT checklist for ISIS-2 1988 — Gold standard RCT

article.title: Randomised trial of intravenous streptokinase, oral aspirin, both, or neither among 17 187 cases of suspected acute myocardial infarction: ISIS-2

study_type.notes: Landmark factorial RCT with 17,187 patients, placebo-controlled, 2x2 design for streptokinase and aspirin in acute MI. Gold standard trial demonstrating both treatments reduce mortality.

1a.notes: Title explicitly states: 'RANDOMISED TRIAL OF INTRAVENOUS STREPTOKINASE, ORAL ASPIRIN, BOTH, OR NEITHER'

1b.notes: Summary includes trial design (factorial), methods (17,187 patients in 417 hospitals), results (mortality reduction), conclusions

2a.notes: Introduction establishes rationale: benefit of thrombolysis and antiplatelet therapy in MI, prior evidence from smaller trials

2b.notes: Objectives: to evaluate effects of streptokinase, aspirin, combination, or neither on 5-week vascular mortality

3a.notes: Trial design clearly described: 2x2 factorial design, placebo-controlled, allocation ratio 1:1:1:1 between four groups

3b.notes: No mention of protocol amendments after trial commencement; paper does not address this item

4a.notes: Eligibility criteria: suspected acute myocardial infarction within 24 hours of onset (median 5 hours); age 24-75 or older without age limit

4b.notes: Settings: 417 hospitals across multiple countries in the International Study of Infarct Survival

5.notes: Interventions detailed: (i) 1-hour IV infusion of 1.5 MU streptokinase; (ii) 160 mg/day aspirin for 1 month (enteric-coated); (iii) both; (iv) placebo for both

6a.notes: Primary outcome: 5-week vascular mortality. Secondary outcomes: reinfarction, stroke, bleeding complications, discharge from hospital

6b.notes: No mention of changes to outcomes after trial commenced; appears pre-specified

7a.notes: Sample size calculation: power calculation described to detect mortality reduction; 17,187 patients enrolled for adequate power

7b.notes: Interim analyses and stopping rules mentioned: steering committee reviewed results periodically

8a.notes: Random sequence generation: described as randomization method; specific method (computer-generated vs. tables) not fully detailed

8b.notes: Type of randomization: 2x2 factorial design with separate randomization for streptokinase and aspirin using stratification by participating center

9.notes: Allocation concealment: sequential randomization described; local hospital staff received allocation via communication with coordinating center

10.notes: Implementation: randomization conducted at clinical centers; local staff enrolled participants and received group assignment

11a.notes: Blinding: double-blind described; patients and staff blinded to streptokinase vs. placebo and aspirin vs. placebo

11b.notes: Similarity of interventions: streptokinase vs. placebo infusion both given as IV infusions; aspirin vs. placebo as identical tablets

12a.notes: Statistical methods: comparison of proportions using odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals; chi-square tests; stratified analysis

12b.notes: Additional analyses: subgroup analyses by time to treatment (0-4, 5-12, 13-24 hours), age, sex; sensitivity analyses excluding some centers

13a.notes: Flow diagram: no explicit CONSORT flow diagram shown; numbers of enrolled, randomized, analyzed reported in text but not in diagram format

13b.notes: Losses and exclusions: 311 patients excluded after randomization (contraindications); 17,187 randomized and analyzed

14a.notes: Recruitment period: August 1986 to July 1987; follow-up through day 35 post-randomization

14b.notes: Trial ended: target enrollment achieved; results frozen when planned number reached

15.notes: Baseline characteristics: Table 1 reports demographic and clinical characteristics by treatment group (age, sex, blood pressure, heart rate, time to treatment)

16.notes: Numbers analyzed: intention-to-treat principle used; all 17,187 randomized patients included in analysis

17a.notes: Primary outcome results: 5-week vascular mortality reported with risk ratios and 95% CIs for each comparison (e.g., streptokinase 791/8592 [9.2%] vs. 1029/8595 [12.0%])

17b.notes: Both relative and absolute effects reported: odds reductions with percentages (25% SD 4, 23% SD 4) and absolute mortality differences

18.notes: Subgroup analyses by time to treatment, age group, sex; sensitivity analyses excluding specific countries; interaction tests between treatments

19.notes: Harms reported: streptokinase associated with excess bleeds requiring transfusion (0.5% vs. 0.2%), cerebral hemorrhage (0.1% vs. 0.0%); aspirin reduced non-fatal reinfarction and stroke

20.notes: Limitations discussed: open-label nature for non-infusion component, inability to assess long-term effects, population selection from hospitals

21.notes: Generalizability: applicability to acute MI patients presenting within 24 hours; primarily studied Western populations; considerations for different patient groups

22.notes: Interpretation: findings support use of both streptokinase and aspirin for acute MI; benefits outweigh harms; additive effects of combination therapy

23.notes: Trial registration: not applicable (1988 publication pre-dates clinical trial registries like ClinicalTrials.gov)

24.notes: Protocol availability: full protocol not described as available in paper; supplementary materials not mentioned

25.notes: Funding: supported by Medical Research Council; funders' role in design/analysis/publication not explicitly stated but appears independent
